title: Error verifying signature: invalid reference format
body:
So I wanted to sign an image and verify it using skopeo in a container. To do so I first created a gpg key fingerprint using the steps described here: 

**https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/6/html/security_guide/sect-security_guide-encryption-gpg-creating_gpg_keys_using_the_command_line**

I then ran: 

skopeo copy --dest-tls-verify=false --remove-signatures docker://docker.io/busybox:latest dir:/src/docker_mount/test/busybox:latest

To sign it I ran:

**skopeo standalone-sign /src/docker_mount/busybox:latest/manifest.json /src/docker_mount/busybox:latest FF9CFA884574566A118B214514255E6EE6F2EA0A -o /src/docker_mount/busybox.signature**

Just to reconfirm the fingerprint I used I ran: 

**gpg2 --fingerprint siddhanthd@gmail.com**

To verify the signed image I ran:

**skopeo standalone-verify /src/docker_mount/busybox:latest/manifest.json /src/docker_mount/busybox:latest FF9CFA884574566A118B214514255E6EE6F2EA0A /src/docker_mount/busybox.signature**

That's when I get the error: **FATA[0000] Error verifying signature: invalid reference format**

![skopeo_sign_verify](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19501855/106438502-960c5b00-649c-11eb-8345-84a315c90261.png)


Can someone tell me what went wrong?

Also, the next thing I wanted to understand was how to use skopeo standalone-verify on existing redhat images. 

**https://catalog.redhat.com/software/containers/rhel/57ea8cee9c624c035f96f3b3?container-tabs=security**

Above is a link to a redhat linux image. Which has a signing code "**199E2F91FD431D51**" displayed on the website.

![linux_image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19501855/106439707-fea80780-649d-11eb-8a7c-9dc98f5fba2d.png)

How do I use this information to verify the image using skopeo. Even to use the skopeo standalone-verify command I'd need a 40 digit fingerprint, and signature file for image. Can someone share some light on this too? Thank you

comments.body:
Thanks for your report.

The “docker-reference” parameter to `standalone-sign` and `standalone-verify` is not a path; it’s the signed image identity, something like `registry.mycompany.example/some-repo/busybox:v1.23`.

---

Usually, you shouldn’t be using `standalone-verify` at all; instead, set up a policy using `policy.json`, and use `skopeo copy`, `podman pull` or the like.

If you do need to use `standalone-verify` for some reason, the individual signature files are necessary. The simplest way to obtain them for published images is to set up the sigstores so that Skopeo can find the signatures (see below), and then use `skopeo copy docker://… dir:…`; the signatures will be copied to the destination directory.

---

WRT accessing the Red Hat signatures, see e.g. https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/8/html/building_running_and_managing_containers/working-with-container-images_building-running-and-managing-containers#defining-the-image-signature-verification-policy_building-running-and-managing-containers .

Thank you for the promt response @mtrmac. I'm gonna try both ways, and post my findings here. Once I set policy.json, and. yaml files, **skopeo copy**, won't yet give me any kind of verification message right? In the link it says **podman run**, would only run fine, if the public key matches with the signature. However I would like to get a verification message only using skopeo, to keep my container lightweight. I'll do the above steps, and then try using the **standalone-verify** command and post the results here.

Hey @mtrmac! So I tried what you suggested above with a Red Hat image. And surely after setting the policy.json, and .yaml files right, skopeo copy verified the signatures and only then copied the image along with the signatures to my desired directory. 

![signatures](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19501855/106738091-88daa200-663d-11eb-8769-492d57222a80.png)

But my requirement was to verify an image in the registry itself and not copy or pull it. For that I will have to use skopeo standalone-verify. So I ran the following command (I used the signature that got downloaded through skopeo copy in my local directory. I used the fingerprint I found here "https://www.redhat.com/security/data/fd431d51.txt" which is the key used to sign Red Hat images, and that also worked with skopeo copy earlier)

**skopeo standalone-verify /src/docker_mount/ubi:8.0-199/manifest.json docker://registry.access.redhat.com/ubi8/ubi:8.0-199 567E347AD0044ADE55BA8A5F199E2F91FD431D51 /src/docker_mount/ubi:8.0-199/signature-1**

This still gave me the same error: **FATA[0000] Error verifying signature: invalid reference format** 

Then when I made a slight change to the referencing of the image:

**skopeo standalone-verify /src/docker_mount/ubi:8.0-199/manifest.json registry.access.redhat.com/ubi8/ubi:8.0-199 567E347AD0044ADE55BA8A5F199E2F91FD431D51 /src/docker_mount/ubi:8.0-199/signature-1**

The "invalid reference format" error went. However it lead me to a new error: Error verifying signature: Invalid GPG signature

![invalid_gpg_key](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19501855/106749577-b4b15400-664c-11eb-8edd-8f4d2f31a4b2.png)

I tried the standalone-verify command with all the signatures that got downloaded in the directory shown above. But all gave out the same error. I can't seem to understand why, cause the image did get copied after verification earlier. Let me know if you understand why this would happen? Also, would you have any idea as to why multiple signatures would get copied for one image?

> But my requirement was to verify an image in the registry itself and not copy or pull it.

(Careful: What is your threat model? The registry can change the served image any time, so a point-in-time check is not  useful if the signatures are to project against compromised registries.)


> For that I will have to use skopeo standalone-verify.

(Well, you _can_ use `skopeo copy` to a temporary location and delete that copy. That allows using the full power of `policy.json` without having to reimplement it — especially if you are using `skopeo copy` anyway to get the signature data; the data is being copied anyway, might as well use that to enforce the policy.)

> **skopeo standalone-verify /src/docker_mount/ubi:8.0-199/manifest.json registry.access.redhat.com/ubi8/ubi:8.0-199 567E347AD0044ADE55BA8A5F199E2F91FD431D51 /src/docker_mount/ubi:8.0-199/signature-1**

This looks correct.

> The "invalid reference format" error went. However it lead me to a new error: Error verifying signature: Invalid GPG signature
> 
> ![invalid_gpg_key](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19501855/106749577-b4b15400-664c-11eb-8edd-8f4d2f31a4b2.png)

Is the relevant GPG key imported into GPG?

> Also, would you have any idea as to why multiple signatures would get copied for one image?

Because there _are_ multiple signatures, in this case signing various references=identities. (So, `standalone-verify` will only report a match for one of the signatures.)
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Assuming there are no more outstanding questions; if that’s not the case, please reopen.
